coredata.dc:title: Efficient sulfadiazine degradation via in-situ epitaxial grow of Graphitic Carbon Nitride (g-C3N4) on carbon dots heterostructures under visible light irradiation: Synthesis, mechanisms and toxicity evaluation
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Duan, Yi
Deng, Lin
Shi, Zhou
Liu, Xia
Zeng, Hanxuan
Zhang, Haojie
Crittenden, John
coredata.dc:description:
The synthesis of environmental-friendly metal-free photocatalysts has great significance in photocatalytic technology. In this work, we firstly report the successful synthesis of in situ epitaxial growth of g-C3N4 on carbon dots through a facile thermal polymerization technique. Characterization and density functional theory (DFT) calculations were conducted to clarify the structure engineering and the electronic/chemical properties of the in-plane interconnected carbon dots/g-C3N4 (C-CN) heterostructures. With the optimal carbon dots content, the C-CN exhibited 3.2 times higher degradation rate for sulfadiazine (SDZ) than that of g-C3N4. Besides, the C-CN heterostructures displayed excellent stability and reusability in five consecutive cycles. The enhanced photocatalytic activity was related to the narrowed band gap and the local electronic density of valance band and conduction band orbitals of the unique plane heterostructures, corroborated by the spectroscopic characterizations and theoretical calculations. Photogenerated holes dominated the degradation of SDZ, while
OH showed a negligible contribution. Moreover, DFT calculation succeeded to predict that the atoms with high Fukin index (f 0) on SDZ molecule were more vulnerable to radicals attack. SDZ degradation pathway mainly included smiles-type rearrangement, SO2 extrusion, ring hydroxylation and SN bond cleavage processes. The eco-toxicity assessment revealed the generation of less toxic intermediates after photocatalysis. Our findings not only afford a new technique for constructing g-C3N4-based in-plane heterostructures with high and stable photocatalytic efficiency, but also highlight the feasible application of metal-free photocatalysts in environmental remediation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
g-C3N4
Epitaxial grow
In-plane heterostructures
Sulfadiazine degradation

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Degradation pathway of SDZ, DFT calculation and eco-toxicity evaluation

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of g-C3N4; (b) EDS mapping of 3C-CN (The inset of TEM image shows the corresponding region for the mapping); and (c) Synthetic route for the C-CN in-plane heterostructures.

(a) XRD patterns of g-C3N4 and 3C-CN, together with the enlarged view of (0 0 2) peak; (b) FTIR spectrum of g-C3N4 and 3C-CN; XPS spectra of for g-C3N4 and 3C-CN: (c) total survey, (d) C 1s and (e) N 1s; (f) Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms of g-C3N4, 0.1C-CN, 1C-CN, 3C-CN and 5C-CN.

(a) Degradation of SDZ by different photocatalysts under visible light irradiation; (b) Kinetic rate constant of SDZ degradation over different photocatalysts; (c) Cycling tests of the 3C-CN towards SDZ degradation; (d) FTIR spectrum of 3C-CN before and after 5 cycles of photocatalytic degradation; XPS spectra of (e) C 1s and (f) N 1s for 3C-CN before and after 5 cycles of photocatalytic degradation.

(a) UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra, (b) PL spectra, and (c) Transient photocurrent response curves of the as-prepared photocatalysts; (d) the electrochemistry properties of the as-prepared photocatalysts: impedance spectra with the corresponding equivalent circuit models; (e) Time-resolved photoluminescence (TR-PL) spectra of g-C3N4 and 3C-CN.

The band structures and corresponding Kohn–Sham orbitals of (a) pristine g-C3N4 and (b) C-CN. The pink and green electron cloud represent the conduction band orbitals and valence band orbitals, respectively. The isosurface are drawn for a charge density of 0.05e/Å3.

ESR spectra recorded at ambient temperature for (a) DMPO-
O2 – adducts and (b) DMPO-
OH adducts in suspension of g-C3N4 and 3C-CN; (c) Effect of different scavengers on the photocatalytic degradation of SDZ; (d) room temperature ESR spectra of g-C3N4 and 3C-CN.
The proposed mechanism for SDZ degradation over 3C-CN in-plane heterostructures under visible light irradiation.

(a) SDZ structure; (b) NPA charge distribution and Fukin index of SDZ; and (c) Photocatalytic degradation pathways of SDZ over 3C-CN (The solid line represents the major pathway, while the dashed line denotes the minor pathway).

head.ce:title: Efficient sulfadiazine degradation via in-situ epitaxial grow of Graphitic Carbon Nitride (g-C3N4) on carbon dots heterostructures under visible light irradiation: Synthesis, mechanisms and toxicity evaluation

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Building Safety and Energy Efficiency
Department of Water Engineering and Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
The synthesis of environmental-friendly metal-free photocatalysts has great significance in photocatalytic technology. In this work, we firstly report the successful synthesis of in situ epitaxial growth of g-C3N4 on carbon dots through a facile thermal polymerization technique. Characterization and density functional theory (DFT) calculations were conducted to clarify the structure engineering and the electronic/chemical properties of the in-plane interconnected carbon dots/g-C3N4 (C-CN) heterostructures. With the optimal carbon dots content, the C-CN exhibited 3.2 times higher degradation rate for sulfadiazine (SDZ) than that of g-C3N4. Besides, the C-CN heterostructures displayed excellent stability and reusability in five consecutive cycles. The enhanced photocatalytic activity was related to the narrowed band gap and the local electronic density of valance band and conduction band orbitals of the unique plane heterostructures, corroborated by the spectroscopic characterizations and theoretical calculations. Photogenerated holes dominated the degradation of SDZ, while
OH showed a negligible contribution. Moreover, DFT calculation succeeded to predict that the atoms with high Fukin index (f 0) on SDZ molecule were more vulnerable to radicals attack. SDZ degradation pathway mainly included smiles-type rearrangement, SO2 extrusion, ring hydroxylation and SN bond cleavage processes. The eco-toxicity assessment revealed the generation of less toxic intermediates after photocatalysis. Our findings not only afford a new technique for constructing g-C3N4-based in-plane heterostructures with high and stable photocatalytic efficiency, but also highlight the feasible application of metal-free photocatalysts in environmental remediation.

ce:section.ce:para: Sulfonamides antibiotics have been extensively used as therapeutic agents for treating diseases causing from bacterial infections in recent decades [1]. Nevertheless, sulfonamides antibiotics are frequently discharged into natural environment via wastewater irrigation, manure and biosolids [2]. The entry of antibiotics into the aquatic ecosystem becomes an emerging issue due to the characteristics of high solubility, chemical stability, and non-biodegradability [3,4]. This definitely exert a severe threat to the environment and human health. Thus, it is desirable to explore an alternative technique for sulfonamides residues decontamination ahead of their discharge into the environment.

Various treatment approaches including adsorption [5], membrane separation [6], coagulation [7], advanced oxidation processes [8], aerobic and anaerobic degradation [9] have been developed for removing antibiotics from wastewater. However, these methods often suffer from unsatisfactory removal efficiency, high cost, secondary pollution and time-consuming [10]. As an adaptive green technique, photocatalysis using semiconductors has received massive attention [11]. The photosensitive semiconductor-based photocatalytic, photoelectrochemical, photovoltaic systems have been established for renewable solar-energy conversion to address the energy consumption and the increasing pollution issues we face today [12–14].

In the past decade, graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), a metal-free photocatalytic material with narrow band gap, has emerged as an ideal candidates in numerous application such as water splitting [15,16], organic contaminants degradation [17,18], adsorption [19], supercapacitors [20], lithium-ion batteries [21], etc. The visible-light-response property, easily preparation, and thermal and chemical stability have made g-C3N4 potentially as a breakthrough in photocatalysis [22–24]. Unfortunately, although the progress in the synthetic routes, the photocatalytic efficiency of g-C3N4 was still restricted by the rapid recombination rate of electron-hole pairs, high defect density, and low surface area. Accordingly, various modification approaches have been explored to build up the photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4 through elements doping (Ag, Pt, Au, etc.) [23,25] and constructing heterojunctions with rare metals, metal oxides or sulfides [26–28].

Metal-free materials with the advantages of non-toxicity, low cost, and great stability have exhibited a capability for further improving the visible-light response of g-C3N4. Carbon dots, as a new carbon-based nanomaterial predominantly composed of graphic carbon (sp2 carbon), showed excellent up-conversion photoluminescence (PL) behavior and remarkable photogenerated charge carrier transfer and reservoir properties [29,30]. Significant achievements have been made on the construction of metal-free g-C3N4 based photocatalysts for improved photocatalytic activity via carbon dots deposition. The obtained two-dimensional layer-by-layer stacking heterostructures have been demonstrated to have an increased visible-light absorption region, diminished photogenerated carrier recombination rate, and enhanced photocatalytic efficiency. Yet, the stacking orientation cannot be accurately controlled, and the interface between layers can be readily polluted [31]. Worse still, obvious recombination of the photocarrier in the interlayer space still happens because of the weak van der Waals driving force between layers. In contrast, a strong in-building electric field induced by in-plane heterostructures would automatically guide ordered movement of photogenerated carriers to suitable redox sites, which would definitely lead to more efficient and rapid transfer of charge carriers [31]. More importantly, few work has been reported to study the inferior correlation of the structure engineering of the as-synthesized g-C3N4-based photocatalysts.

Herein, we report an in situ epitaxial growth strategy for the construction of in-plane interconnected carbon dots/g-C3N4 (C-CN) heterostructures. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations were applied to investigate the inferior correlation of the structure engineering and the electronic/chemical properties of the novel metal-free photocatalyst. Photocatalytic experiments suggested that the as-prepared hexagonal-like C-CN exhibits much higher visible-light-driven photocatalytic activity toward sulfadiazine (SDZ) degradation compared with that of the g-C3N4. The morphology, crystal structure, chemical composition, electronic structure of the as-prepared C-CN, and radical identification were characterized in detail to clarify the underlying mechanism for the improved photocatalytic activity. The degradation pathway of SDZ was speculated using both intermediates recognition and theoretical calculations based on DFT Fukin index. In addition, the eco-toxicity of the degradation intermediates of SDZ were evaluated via Ecological Structure Activity Relationships (ECOSAR) program for the first time.
This section is provided in Text S1, S2, S3 and S4 in Supplementary Material.

Density functional theory (DFT) calculations were conducted using the Vienna ab initio simulation package (VASP). The Perdew–Burke–Ernzerhof generalized gradient approximation (GGA) was adopted for the exchange and correlation functional. A vacuum region of 15 Å was added to avoid the interactions between adjacent images. A plane wave cut-off energy of 450 eV and the Gaussian smearing with 0.01 eV broadening were employed to guarantee the convergence. The Brillouin-zone is sampled by gamma-centered Monkhorst–Pack method with 3 × 3 × 1 k-point mesh for geometric optimizations and by that of 9 × 9 × 1 k-point mesh for the electronic property calculation. At the end of structure optimizations with forces less than 0.01 eV Å−1, the electron localization function was calculated. As the structure is diverse and the Zigzag-edge is more stable than armchair edge of in carbon dots [31,32], therefore, we built the C-CN heterostructures with melem units epitaxial growth around a regular hexagon carbon dot. The carbon dot was exposed with six Zigzag edge including 96 C atoms. The structures of pristine g-C3N4 and C-CN in-plane heterostructures are illustrated in Fig. S1a and b, respectively.

Density functional theory (DFT) calculations were conducted using the Vienna ab initio simulation package (VASP). The Perdew–Burke–Ernzerhof generalized gradient approximation (GGA) was adopted for the exchange and correlation functional [33]. A plane wave cut-off energy of 450 eV and the Gaussian smearing with 0.01 eV broadening were employed to guarantee the convergence. The K-points sampling set of 3 × 3 × 1 was good enough for the supercell calculations. At the end of structure optimizations with forces less than 0.01 eV Å−1, the electron localization function was calculated.

The surface morphologies of as-synthesized photocatalysts was studied by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. S2, the TEM image reveals a diameter of ca. 3 nm for carbon dots. While the g-C3N4 exhibits a distinct sheets-like morphology with smooth surface (Fig. 1 a) with no obvious nanoparticles existence. From Fig. 1b, C and N signals are observed in the 3C-CN, rooting in g-C3N4 domains. With regard to the marked elliptic region, abundance of C is obviously seen. In view of carbon dots as the reactant, the abundant C signals can be attributed to the in-plane carbon ring, signifying that 3C-CN are in-plane heterostructures constituted of g-C3N4 and C-rings. Moreover, the as-synthesized 3C-CN still holds the primary structural characteristics of g-C3N4 after the incorporation of carbon dots as proved by XRD analysis (Fig. 2 a). A proposed synthesis process is schematically illustrated in Fig. 1c based on the DFT calculation as previously discussed.

Fig. 2a illustrates the XRD patterns of g-C3N4 and 3C-CN. Apparently, two distinct diffraction peaks at 13.1° and 27.4° corresponds to (1 0 0) and (0 0 2) planes of g-C3N4, respectively. This typically refers the in-plane packing of tri-s-triazine units and the interlayer conjugated aromatic system stacking, respectively [11,34]. After the incorporation of carbon dots, the (0 0 2) reflection peak shifts from 27.4° to 28.0°, suggesting smaller interlayer distance of 3C-CN. It’s expected that the decreased interplanar distance of g-C3N4 would shorten the charge transfer space from the bulk to the surface, which definitely benefits the photocatalytic activity of the material [35]. FTIR spectra of g-C3N4 and 3C-CN in Fig. 2b represent similar characteristic vibration modes, indicating the maintenance of the tri-s-triazine-based structure of g-C3N4 in 3C-CN [36]. The bands at 1640 (CN), 1572 (CN), 1406 (CN), 1395 (CN), 1316 (CN), and 1238 cm−1 (CN) can be assigned to the typical CN heterocycles stretching modes [35]. The band at 811 cm−1 corresponds to the out-of-plane vibration of both triazine and heptazine rings [37,38]. The broad absorption peaks at 3000–3400 cm−1 can be ascribed to the stretching vibration modes for NH of g-C3N4 and surface adsorbed hydroxyl species [35,39].

Moreover, we performed XPS measurements to probe chemical states of elements in the resultant materials. From Fig. 2c, g-C3N4 shows C 1s and N 1s signals with an N/C ratio of 1.33, corresponding to the ideal g-C3N4 composition reported in previous literature [40,41]. Also, the high-resolution C 1s spectrum was deconvoluted to clarify the chemical bonding between the carbon and nitrogen atoms in g-C3N4 and 3C-CN (Fig. 2d and e). The peaks centered at the binding energies of 288.3 and 284.8 eV corresponds to the sp2–bonded carbon (NCN) and graphitic carbon (CC/CC), respectively. These assignments agree well with those previous reported for g-C3N4 powder [41,42]. The only difference in the spectrum between the studied samples is the appearance of a new peak at 286.2 eV related to the CO bond in 3C-CN [43]. Fig. 2e represents the high-resolution N 1s spectrum for g-C3N4, and it displays four typical deconvoluted peaks at 398.8, 399.6, 401.1 and 404.8 eV, corresponding to the sp2–bonded N (CNC), tertiary N in N(C)3 or HN(C)2, amino groups (NH), and π excitations, respectively [43]. The increased intensity of N(C)3 groups is because of the raised amount of N(C)3 groups in 3C-CN, and this evidences the generation of the pyridine ring structure resulted from the reaction between carbon dots and tri-s-triazine as depicted in Fig. S2c. Moreover, element analysis was done to compare the C/N atomic ratio of g-C3N4 and 3C-CN. As we can see in Table S1, the C/N atomic ratio of g-C3N4 was determined to be 0.670, while the ratio of 3C-CN was slightly increased to 0.678 due to the introduction of carbon dots.

Specific surface area plays a significant role in the adsorption behavior of materials, which can be influence the photocatalytic efficiency of photocatalysts. Thus, N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms were performed to assess the BET surface area (SBET) of g-C3N4 and C-CN heterostructures (Figs. 2f and S3). As we can see, all samples display type IV curves with a H3-type hysteresis loops over a high relative pressure, revealing the mesoporous structure [4]. The calculated SBET value of the g-C3N4 is 78.9 m2/g, highlighting its high porous structure. The SBET values of 0.5C-CN, 1C-CN, 3C-CN and 5C-CN are determined to be 71.2, 72.9, 75.5 and 77.0 m2/g, respectively. The introduction of carbon dots did not lead to a significant change in the specific surface area of g-C3N4.

Fig. S4 illustrates the Raman spectra of g-C3N4 and 3C-CN. Characteristic peaks at 473, 708, 753, 769, 979, 1116, 1233, and 1313 cm−1 related to the pristine g-C3N4 can be observed in two samples [44,45]. Particularly, the peaks centered at 708 and 1233 cm−1 are ascribed to the breathing mode of heptazine ring and the stretching vibration modes of CN and CN heterocycles, respectively [46,47]. Compared to the g-C3N4, the 3C-CN shows two distinct peaks at 1357 cm−1 (D band) and 1573 cm−1 (G band), further corroborating the existence of carbon dots. Besides, the band intensity of 3C-CN is higher than that of g-C3N4 probably owing to the improved charge carriers transfer between carbon dots and tri-s-triazine [44].

The photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4 and various C-CN heterostructures was evaluated toward SDZ degradation (Fig. 3 a). In dark condition, less than 10% of SDZ is removed in 30 min, implying that the adsorption capacity of the samples can be neglected. In the absence of photocatalysts, direct photocatalysis of SDZ can also be ignored. Yet, with the addition of photocatalysts, obvious SDZ degradation is achieved. For g-C3N4, 53.7% of SDZ is degraded in 80 min. While the as-synthesized C-CN in-plane heterostructures exhibit enhanced visible-light photocatalytic degradation efficiency. As we can see, removal efficiency of SDZ by 0.5C-CN, 1C-CN, 3C-CN and 5C-CN over the same timeline are 62.7%, 75.2%, 92.8% and 85.7%, respectively. The photocatalytic efficiency of C-CN is increased along with the increase of carbon dots mass content; while further increasing carbon dots mass decreases the photocatalytic efficiency. The excess amount of carbon dots will lead to a shield effect and inhibit photocatalysis. Hence, 3C-CN is chosen as the best photocatalyst in this work. Fig. 3b depicts the kinetic rate constant of SDZ degradation by different photocatalysts. Optimum activity is obtained with 3C-CN (0.032 min−1), and it is 3.2-fold larger than the pristine g-C3N4 (0.010 min−1).

Experiments were also done to study the influence of solution pH on the photocatalytic activity of the as-synthesized material. As depicted in Fig. S5, the photocatalyst displayed the highest SDZ removal at pH = 5.7, and lower or higher pH value was not favorable for SDZ removal. When the solution pH increased, according to the hydrogen ion's Nernst equation (E = Eθ-0.059pH), the valence band of 3C-CN moved up, thus weakening the oxidation capacity of the photocatalyst. While SDZ existed in forms of SDZ molecules and SDZ+ in acid conditions [48]. Compared to SDZ molecules or SDZ+, SDZ− can be more easily oxidized [49]. Thus, the SDZ degradation declined to some extent at pH = 4.0. In the view of actual application, it is an advantage that the as-synthesized photocatalyst can be used without the need of pH adjustment as the pH value of most natural waters are neutral.

Aside from the impressive photocatalytic efficiency, the 3C-CN also possesses superb photocatalytic stability during repeated utilization as depicted in Fig. 3c. After five consecutive cycles, 91.1% of SDZ is degraded in 80 min, which is comparable to that of the first run (92.8%). On the other hand, the unobvious change in the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 3d) and XPS spectra of C 1s (Fig. 3e) and N 1s (Fig. 3f) is also greatly supportive of the outstanding stability and durability of the 3C-CN.

Experiments were done to evaluate the degradation efficiency of SDZ in water samples of tap water, river water and lake water (Fig. S6). As we can see, the degradation efficiency achieved in tap water, river water and lake water were 85.9%, 83.4%, and 79.9%, respectively. The chemical oxygen demand (COD) in tap water, river water and lake water were 9.3, 13.7 and 27.1 mg/L, respectively. It’s possible that the decrease in SDZ degradation was related to the chemical oxygen demand (COD) as the decomposition of COD consumed reactive species produced by 3C-CN. But after all, the SDZ degradation achieved by 3C-CN in the studied waters was high.
Consequently, it is convictive that the 3C-CN with exceptional photocatalytic property can be a promising visible-light-driven photocatalyst for environmental remediation.

UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) was conducted to investigate the optimum absorption properties of the samples, and the results are illustrated in Fig. 4 a. Just like other report, g-C3N4 synthesized in this work exhibits a markedly absorption band having a characteristic edge at approximately 470 nm. Also, compared to the pure g-C3N4, the light absorption capability over the entire wavelength range is greatly improved upon the increased mass content of carbon dots. This observation is related to the inherent characteristics of carbon dots. The inset paragraph represents the color change of C-CNs when varying the carbon dots mass contents. The photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra was carried out to disclose the charge carriers’ transfer and recombination efficiency of the photocatalysts (Fig. 4b). As we know, the recombination rate of the photoinduced carriers depends highly on the emission intensity [50]. A high PL intensity generally indicates a serious combination rate of electron-hole pairs [35]. The figure shows that the pristine g-C3N4 possesses much higher fluorescence emission peak at 450–550 nm, owing to a severe electron-hole recombination. While the PL intensity is decreased sharply for C-CN in-plane heterostructures, revealing the suppressed recombination rate of the photoinduced carriers [51,52]. Furthermore, as discussed in Text S5 and Fig. S7, the accurate band gap E g of 3C-CN is 2.74 eV, which is shorter than that of pure g-C3N4 (2.80 eV). The valance band (VB) potential E VB of 3C-CN and g-C3N4 are 1.68 eV (vs. SHE) and 1.66 eV (vs. SHE). Accordingly, the conduction band (CB) potential E CB of g-C3N4 and 3C-CN are −1.14 eV (vs. SHE) and −1.06 eV (vs. SHE) by calculation, respectively (E CB = E VB − E g).

In addition, the photocurrents were measured to identify the separation rate of the photo-generated electron-hole pairs (Fig. 4c). Photocurrent transient response for g-C3N4 is the weakest due to the rapid recombination of electron-hole pairs. While the 3C-CN exhibits the highest photocurrent response among the studied photocatalysts, suggesting that the interfacial charge transfer rate of 3C-CN is faster and thereby generates more electrons. The electron transfer barrier is discussed using EIS analysis. As depicted in Fig. 4d, the 3C-CN displays the smallest arc radius, demonstrating the lowered resistance and promoted charge separation and transfer efficiency. The trend in the figure further proves that an appropriate loading of carbon dots is required; a lower or higher amount of carbon dots may cause a slower transfer of photo-generated electrons. This is also in accordance with the photocatalytic performance evaluation results.

To further probe charge transfer of the as-synthesized samples, we carried out a TR-PL spectra of g-C3N4 and 3C-CN. By exponential fitting, the charge-carrier lifetimes (<τ>) are calculated in Fig. 4e (τ 1 originates from the nonradiative recombination of charge carries, and τ 2 is related to the recombination of free excitons) [53]. Results show that the emission lifetimes of both components of 3C-CN (τ 1 = 1.60, τ 2 = 6.24 ns) are shorter than that of the corresponding g-C3N4 counterpart (τ 1 = 1.76, τ 2 = 7.15 ns). Besides, the average emission lifetime displays an obvious decline for 3C-CN (3.85 ns) as compared with that of g-C3N4 (5.18 ns). The PL analysis and lifetime reduction indicate the establishment of an electron transfer channel from tri-s-triazine units to carbon dots in a nonradiative quenching pathway. Accordingly, this results in effective interfacial charge transfer and inhibition of photoexcited charge recombination in 3C-CN.

In addition, the DFT calculations of electronic structures were performed for a comprehensive knowledge of the relationship between the as-synthesized samples and photocatalytic efficiency. The band structure and corresponding Kohn–Sham orbitals for valence band and conduction band of the pristine g-C3N4 and C-CN are compared in Fig. 5 . Apparently, the g-C3N4 is a typical semiconductor, and the valence and conduction band are mainly ascribed to the 2p orbitals of N and C atoms, respectively. In contrary to the g-C3N4, the band gap of C-CN is declined to some extent, and some new bands appears in the energy window above 0.75 eV. The valance band and conduction band orbitals are mainly located in g-C3N4 and carbon dots, respectively. The narrowed band gap of C-CN corresponds to the expanded visible light absorption and smaller impedance, which agrees well with the results in Fig. 4a and d. The local electronic density of valance band and conduction band orbitals means a more effectively photogenerated electron-hole pairs separation, which is accord with the lower PL emission peak (Fig. 4b) and shortened PL lifetime (Fig. 4e).

To distinguish reactive species involved in the photocatalytic system, we carried out the ESR measurements and quenching experiments by adding different radical scavengers. ESR tests were performed with spin trap DMPO. The time-dependent ESR signals for both g-C3N4 and 3C-CN are represented in Fig. 6 a and b. No ESR signals are found in the dark condition, which is due to the weak reaction between photocatalysts and solvent without the light irradiation. Yet, four-fold typical peaks of DMPO-
OH with an intensity ratio of 1:2:2:1 and DMPO-
O2 – peaks with an intensity ratio of 1:1:1:1 are obtained in the presence of g-C3N4 and 3C-CN under the visible light irradiation. A higher intensity probably comes from the higher generation rates of
O2 – and
OH radicals in 3C-CN system than in the g-C3N4 system. This trend is in consist with the observed degradation performance, and demonstrates that more reactive oxygen species can attack SDZ molecules in the 3C-CN system and subsequently promote their degradation that in the g-C3N4 one.

BQ, IPA, and EDTA-2Na were selected as capture agents of the superoxide radical (
O2 –), hydroxyl radical (
OH) and holes (h+) [54,55]. As illustrated in Fig. 6c, the SDZ decomposition exhibits a certain inhibitory effect with the addition of different reactive species capture agents. When adding 10 mM BQ and 10 mM EDTA-2Na, the SDZ removal efficiency drops sharply from 91.9% to 12.1% and 63.2%, respectively. This reveals that both
O2 – radicals and h+ contributes to SDZ degradation, and h+ plays a much greater role. On the other hand, IPA displays a slight inhibitory effect on SDZ degradation, signifying that
OH radicals plays a minor role in the studied system. On the basis of above analysis, the contribution order of reactive species is h+ >
O2 – >
OH.

Furthermore, the ESR spectra of g-C3N4 and 3C-CN in Fig. 6d was performed to study the extent of conjugation. As we can see, a g value of 2.004 is observed, which is related to the delocalized electrons on sp2 carbon atoms of the aromatic rings [56]. However, the 3C-CN displays a stronger ESR signal than g-C3N4, suggesting a well-established electronic band structure. It’s generally accepted that the electronic band structure of g-C3N4 results from the production of tri-s-triazine units into an extended π-conjugated system. And the stronger ESR intensity reveals that the 3C-CN shows optimized π-conjugated system [57], which is beneficial for electrons transfer.

From the above analysis, we propose a rational photocatalytic mechanism for SDZ degradation over 3C-CN as shown in Fig. 7 . Exposed to the visible light, 3C-CN is excited to produce electrons (e−) in the conduction band, with the concurrent generation of holes (h +) in the valence band [58]. According to the DFT calculation, the excited electrons of the 3C-CN are accumulated mainly on carbon dots thanks to its superior electron conductivity and storage capacity; while holes are gathered on tri-s-triazine of heterostructures. The aggregated h + can destroy SDZ molecule directly because of its high oxidation ability. The accumulated electrons can be quickly trapped by dissolved oxygen to yield
O2 – radicals or H2O2 (O2 + e– =
O2 –, O2 + 2H+ + 2e– = H2O2, respectively) via a one or two-electron reduction process because of the more negative CB potential of 3C-CN (−1.06 eV vs. SHE) [59,60]. Subsequently, the H2O2 species can react with electrons to produce
OH radicals [60]. The photogenerated
O2 – and
OH, together with the remaining holes on 3C-CN can degrade SDZ.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degradation pathway of SDZ, DFT calculation and eco-toxicity evaluation
ce:section.ce:para: To interpret the degradation pathway of SDZ over 3C-CN in-plane heterostructures under visible light irradiation, HPLC/Triple Quad mass spectrometry analysis and computational DFT calculation were performed. Table S2 and Figs. S8–S15 depicts the detected degradation intermediates. The natural population analysis (NPA) charge distribution and Fukin index representative of radical attack (f 0) of atoms on SDZ were carried out on a Gaussian 03 package to further clarify the degradation pathway (Fig. 8 a and b). Details on DFT calculations are clarified in Text S6. According to the results, N1, N3, C8, C9, O1 and O2 atoms hold larger f 0 and are more vulnerable to radicals attack. On the basis of the identified intermediates and Fukin index, the pathway of SDZ degradation is schematically proposed in Fig. 8c.

In pathway I, electron of SDZ is transferred to the hole of the photocatalyst with oxidation potential of 3C-CN, resulting in the generation of an aniline radical cation. Owing to the high f 0 of the N3 (0.0645), O1 (0.047) and O2 (0.048) atoms, this compound can undergo the smiles-type rearrangement and SO2 extrusion to form Product 1 (P1, m/z = 187.2). Subsequently, N1 atom of P1 tends to be attacked by
O2 –, resulting in the production of P2 (m/z = 217.2) [61]. In pathway II, N1 atom with the second highest f 0 can be oxidized directly by
O2 – to generate P4 (m/z = 281.1) [1]. In pathway III, C8 and C9 atoms with the f 0 of 0.042 on SDZ can be easily attacked by
OH to produce hydroxylated SDZ (P3, m/z = 267.1) [62,63]. Additionally, P5 (m/z = 173.2, Sulfanilic acid) and P6 (m/z = 95.9, 2-amino pyrimidine) are produced because of the SN bond cleavage of SDZ molecule due to the attack of
O2 – and
OH [10].

Additionally, ECOSAR program was applied for predicting the toxicity of SDZ and its degradation intermediates. As illustrated in Fig. S16, the Half Lethal Concentration (LC50) of SDZ toward fish and daphnia are determined to be 1516.102 and 1.884 mg/L, and the Half Effective Concentration (EC50) of SDZ toward green algae is 10.254 mg/L. The Chronic toxicities (ChVs) toward fish, daphnia and green algae are 8.953, 0.148 and 14.888 mg/L, respectively. Based on the criterion issued by the Globally Harmonized System of Classification and Labelling of Chemicals (United Nations, 2011), SDZ can be sorted as a very toxic organic contaminant. Thus, the intermediates toxicity should be evaluated. Fig. S16 depicts that the acute and chronic toxicity of all the intermediates to three typical aquatic species are less (except the 96hr LC50 values of P1, P2, P3, P4 and P6) than that of SDZ. This implies that the photocatalytic degradation of SDZ is very momentous for the aquatic environment safety.

In summary, we reported the in-situ epitaxial growth of g-C3N4 on carbon dots for the construction of a novel C-CN in-plane heterostructures via a facile thermal polymerization method. Notably, the resultant C-CN exhibited significantly improved photocatalytic efficiency towards the elimination of sulfadiazine under visible light irradiation in contrast to g-C3N4. The spectroscopic and photoelectrochemical analysis and DFT calculations revealed that the enhanced photocatalytic performance was attributed to the improved photogenerated electron-hole pair’s separation, enhanced light harvesting ability, as well as the shortened charge-transfer time. Moreover, the C-CN heterostructures displayed outstanding photocatalytic stability after five consecutive cycles without obvious loss. The radical scavenge tests and ESR spectra validated that h+ played dominant role, while
OH radicals showed a negligible contribution. The atoms with high f 0 values interpret the smiles-type rearrangement, SO2 extrusion, ring hydroxylation and SN bond cleavage with the attack of reactive species. The eco-toxicity of SDZ was alleviated during the photocatalysis based on the prediction using ECOSAR program. This work not only provides new ideas for the novel metal-free g-C3N4-based in-plane heterostructures, but also guarantees a capable photocatalyst for effective decontamination of emerging contaminants.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51808206 and 51878256), Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province (2018JJ3062), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities. The authors also appreciate the support from the Brook Byers Institute for Sustainable Systems, Hightower Chair and the Georgia Research Alliance at Georgia Institute of Technology.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2019.11.046.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
